author: Jack | Will Never Message You First
msg:
[Media] The error means that there was an issue validating the integrity of the device. This could be caused by anything that modifies system-level services.

Team's suggestion is a factory reset and updating to the latest android version possible for your device. 

- Form via WEB: https://unitynodes.io/bug-report/
- Telegram BOT: https://t.me/UnityBugReportBot
- Email: support@unitynodes.io

author: Crg878
msg:
I was hoping to get some advice or see if anyone else has experienced something similar. I’ve got an iPhone 13 that’s running at around 93% uptime. Not amazing compared to some of my Android devices (which are hitting closer to 98%), but still fairly stable. However, my wife’s identical iPhone 13,  with the same settings, behaves completely differently. It struggles to stay connected and often disconnects within minutes. We’re lucky if it reaches 9% uptime. It’s really puzzling how two identical devices can perform so differently under the same conditions. I’ve submitted a bug report and was told it’s a known issue being worked on, but I’m curious if anyone here has experienced this and found any kind of workaround or fix, it was ok untill about two weeks ago, then just changed……..On another note, I’ve also noticed something interesting with my Android devices. I currently have 7 phones running on the same IP address, each earning roughly between 0.01 and 0.002. However, when I disconnect all of them and leave just one device running, that single phone earns around 0.25 to 0.30, significantly more overall. At the moment, it seems more profitable to just run one device instead of multiple, but I’m unsure how this affects the recalibration process. There’s not much clear information about it, so I wanted to ask: Do devices need to stay connected continuously to be included in recalibration? If devices are turned off, could they miss out on recalibration entirely?
Any insight, experiences, or advice would be greatly appreciated.
Thanks in advance!

author: Compost Man Unity code: XY
msg: I was playing around with AI and this seemed apt ....Telecom is usually very hierarchical and cautious — problems get escalated up the chain before anyone talks about them openly, and everything's documented to death. Crypto's the exact opposite: everything's public by design, people shitpost their failures on Twitter, and speed beats perfection. Mixing the two is brutal because telecom engineers want six months of testing before saying anything, while the crypto side wants to ship next week and let the community find the bugs. The tension usually shows up in how they handle outages — telecom wants to hide them, crypto wants to tweet about them.

author: Compost Man Unity code: XY
msg: People bought the vision but got telecom bureaucracy wrapped in crypto marketing.

author: Charles
msg: Sounds about right

author: Compost Man Unity code: XY
msg: What should we do in terms of our mindset? ... Stay outcome-focused, not timeline-focused. The tech is real, the vision is real — the frustration is almost entirely about speed and communication. Treat delays as a feature of the hybrid model, not a bug. Set personal rules: I check community sentiment once a week max, I don't read price charts daily, and I remind myself this is a five-to-ten-year infrastructure play, not a six-month token flip. The real alpha is being able to sit through the ugly middle phase when retail is screaming but the actual network is quietly building. That's where the separation between tourists and long-term holders happens.

author: Compost Man Unity code: XY
msg: End

author: Sojourner
msg:
My original setup was 7 mini WiFi only Android phone farm that was working great. I added two Apple devices to the WiFi only setup (old iPhone and iPad) as well as 4 family phones that mix sim and WiFi. Since the recalibration, I have removed 6 Android phones from IP and converted one to sim so I only have at most 6 on 1 IP at any given time. Rewards have rebounded back to previous levels for the most part.

I noticed my kids and wife’s phones were not performing well during the week but would uptick on the weekend. I realized they were jumping on the the school WiFi  or work WiFi for a good portion of the day and those tend to block everything. The only way to maintain good uptime is for them to turn off the WiFi while outside the home.

author: Bitdano
msg: Yes, I agree, jumping on and off of Wi-Fi during the day messes up the connectivity.

author: Sojourner
msg: Yep, I tend to avoid jumping onto any public WiFi networks, but my kids add WiFi everywhere they go 🤷‍♂️

author: Krabalot
msg:
Yeah, I get that it's in their best interest to deliver, but it's also in their best interests that existing UNOs and ULOs are engaged. The second part can lead to communication tactics that bring high expectations that often backfire. 

Speaking as an Earth Node holder, the deliveries have been delayed consistently for years, but the communication is always transparent on what the issues are and what they are doing to fix it on an almost instantaneous basis.
